questions.question: Place the founding milestones of eddy current testing in correct chronological order:
options: Faraday's induction law → Arago's rotating disk → Maxwell's equations → Lenz's law → Hughes' induction balance
Arago's rotating disk → Lenz's law → Faraday's induction law → Hughes' induction balance → Maxwell's equations
Arago's rotating disk → Faraday's induction law → Lenz's law → Maxwell's equations → Hughes' induction balance
Maxwell's equations → Faraday's induction law → Lenz's law → Arago's rotating disk → Hughes' induction balance
questions.explanation: Arago saw the unexplained effect (1824), Faraday explained induction (1831), Lenz fixed the direction of induced currents (1834), Maxwell completed the mathematics (1864–1873), and only then did Hughes build the first practical materials test, the induction balance (1879).

questions.question: Hughes' 1879 induction balance could sort metals and coins, yet eddy current testing stalled as a laboratory trick for decades afterward. The main reason was that:
options: the balance responded to alloy, size, shape, temperature, and position alike, with no way to tell the variables apart
metals conductive enough to carry eddy currents had not yet been refined
alternating current sources could not be built until the 1940s
the underlying physics of induction was still unknown
questions.explanation: Hughes' balance announced that something differed but never said what. Responding to every variable at once kept ET impractical until quantitative instruments and impedance-plane analysis solved the separation problem. The physics had been complete since Maxwell's 1873 treatise.

questions.question: Friedrich Förster's work beginning in 1948 is treated as the start of modern eddy current testing because he:
options: invented the first transistorized portable instrument
discovered that eddy currents concentrate at the part surface
developed multifrequency techniques for steam generator tubing
built stable, calibrated, quantitative instruments and introduced impedance-plane analysis to separate test variables
questions.explanation: Förster's instruments gave repeatable, calibrated readings, and impedance-plane analysis let an operator separate conductivity, permeability, lift-off, and crack responses by their distinct directions on the display. Multifrequency tubing work came later from Hugo Libby; transistor portables arrived in the 1960s.

questions.question: The strength of the primary magnetic field produced by a given test coil is set by its:
options: wire diameter and insulation thickness
ampere-turns — the drive current multiplied by the number of turns
test frequency alone
distance from the nearest conductor
questions.explanation: Coiling the wire stacks each turn's field into one concentrated field along the axis, with strength set by ampere-turns: current × turns. Frequency controls how fast the flux changes — which governs induction — not the basic strength of the field.

questions.question: Flux through a conductor can be changed by relative motion (the generator principle) or by varying the current with time (the transformer principle). Eddy current testing creates induction by:
options: varying the coil current — the AC drive does the inducing, while probe motion merely covers the part
sweeping the probe rapidly so a steady field moves across the part
rotating the part beneath a permanent magnet
passing direct current into the part through electrical contact
questions.explanation: An AC-driven coil carries a built-in flux variation — the field expands, collapses, and reverses polarity every cycle — so induction requires no motion at all. Scanning exists for coverage. Arago's spinning disk used the motion route; the modern test does not.

questions.question: An aluminum skin can be eddy current tested straight through its paint layer because:
options: paint becomes weakly conductive under an alternating field
the probe presses through the coating to make electrical contact
the varying magnetic field passes through nonconductors unimpeded — the paint only adds coil-to-metal spacing (liftoff)
eddy currents induced in the paint relay the field to the metal
questions.explanation: Nonconductive coatings carry no eddy currents and do not block the field; energy couples to the metal magnetically, with no contact or couplant. The only price is extra coil-to-metal distance — the liftoff effect — which weakens coupling but still allows inspection of the metal beneath.

questions.question: If a probe coil could be placed on a hypothetical perfect conductor carrying loss-free eddy currents, the coil's impedance would show:
options: increased inductive reactance and increased resistance
reduced inductive reactance with effective resistance unchanged
increased effective resistance with reactance unchanged
no change at all, since no energy is dissipated
questions.explanation: Loss-free eddy currents still cancel part of the primary flux, so flux linkage and Xʟ fall — but with no I²R dissipation, no energy is drawn from the coil circuit and no resistance is added. Real metals have finite conductivity, which is why both effects always appear together.

questions.question: A surface probe is moved from a titanium fitting (only a few percent IACS) to a copper part of much higher conductivity. Compared with titanium, the copper produces:
options: weaker eddy currents, because copper resists induction
the same response — conductivity does not affect the secondary field
no secondary field, because copper is nonmagnetic
stronger eddy currents and a stronger opposing secondary field, so the coil's inductive reactance drops further
questions.explanation: Higher conductivity lets the induced voltage drive larger circulating currents. Their stronger secondary field cancels more of the primary flux, pulling inductive reactance further down than the poorly conducting titanium did. Magnetism is irrelevant here — both metals are nonmagnetic conductors.

questions.question: Placing the probe on a ferromagnetic part moves the coil's inductive reactance in the opposite direction from a nonmagnetic conductor — it rises — because the part:
options: concentrates magnetic flux through its high permeability, in addition to carrying eddy currents
cannot carry eddy currents at all
reverses the direction demanded by Lenz's law
carries eddy currents that reinforce the primary field
questions.explanation: A nonmagnetic conductor only pushes back, lowering flux linkage and Xʟ. A ferromagnetic part also concentrates flux through its high permeability, and that effect outweighs the eddy current opposition, so net inductive reactance rises. Lenz's law still holds — induced currents always oppose the change.

questions.question: During a scan, an eddy current instrument responds directly to:
options: the crack's own magnetic field radiating from the part
current flowing from the part into the probe through electrical contact
changes in the test coil's impedance, produced by changes in the eddy currents' secondary field
the heat generated by eddy currents in the part
questions.explanation: The instrument never sees a flaw. Every signal travels the same chain: a variable disturbs the eddy currents, the altered secondary field changes the net flux at the coil, and the coil's impedance shifts. Cracks, conductivity, liftoff, and thickness all speak through that one chain — which is why a procedure must control the variables the test is not meant to measure.
